sentences.text: Vera Ray, V-E-R-A, and Ray is R-A-Y.
Silbiger.
S-I-L-B-E-G-R.
Yes, my name after the war was Silvan, S-I-L-V-A-N.
And when I got married, my name was Reich, R-E-I-C-H.
So lots of names.
[LAUGHS]
The 10th of the 12th, 1932.
So that's the 10th of December '32.
I was born in Vitkovice, Czechoslovakia.
Oh, I'll try.
V-I-T-K-O-V-I-C-E.
OK.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I have this really strange first memory.
And it actually is my first memory that I can think of.
And that is-- my first memory is actually being told not to tell someone that I'm Jewish.
And I remember sitting on the kitchen table and somebody was putting on my shoes and socks.
And could've been my mother saying, Veraska, when you see Mr.
Shacht, tell him you're not Jewish.
And it must have left an impression on me because it was probably something amazing.
My mother usually made sense.
But that one didn't make sense.
So I think that's my first earliest memory, yeah.
I don't actually.
But maybe he was German or something.
It's-- it's a pretty German sort of a name, isn't it?
Yes, so that was my earliest memory.
There are also some memories which are about people in our house that I wasn't supposed to know about.
I remember light coming out from under the dining room door, and, and movement and voices.
And when I said to my parents in the morning, who was here?
They told me nobody.
And later my father told me that they were people from Ostrava who-- that he was basically rescuing.
He was part of the underground.
Now something to do with-- they divided up Czechoslovakia between the Poles and the Germans.
And we somehow ended up in Poland in Orlova, which was, you know, a few kilometers from Ostrava.
But Ostrava went to the Germans.
And I think the people were crossing the border at night or something like that.
Well, look, I don't know.
But I guess it would have been '38.
Years are very difficult for me because, you know, I was-- I was born in '32.
So in '38 I was six years old.
I can remember the taste of ice cream rather than the years.
But I'll do my best.
In my immediate family?
My mother, my father, and myself.
My father's name was [?
Yindry, ?] which translates as Henry.
And my mother's name was Jolana Silbiger and after the war, Silvan.
Before the war?
Before the war, you know, I think we were a fairly middle class sort of a family.
My mother was well-educated in relation to the educational standards of, of women of her time.
She had-- she did her-- she had a matriculation.
And then she went to a conservatorium of music.
And she ended up being a music teacher and a language teacher.
So she taught German, French, and English.
She ended up knowing nine languages.
One day we counted it up.
It was a bit hard for her when she was getting old because they got a bit confused.
My mother was a very intelligent woman, a very strong willed woman, and the war really ruined her.
She, she ended up suffering from anxiety attacks.
And it was pretty bad.

But at the time, she-- you know, before the war, I remember her sort of, you know, being the lady of the house and-- and giving piano lessons and telling the cook what to cook and the governess what to governess.
And it all seemed quite happy.
That was my mother before the war.
My father was a typical breadwinner.
He-- his main aim in life is to look-- was to look after his family.
He came from a very poor family.
He was the eldest of seven children.
His father was a very poor man.
His name was Solomon, the father's-- my grandfather's name.
And he used to sell-- you know, he was a hawker.
He used to sell, like, shoelaces, and bits and pieces of-- what do you call this thing, you know, like a board and they had it all-- and they walked around with-- with their wares.
Anyway, he was probably the poorest of the poor.
And they were seven children.
My father was the eldest, and he often didn't have much to eat.
So-- but he was very caring, because, you know, he had to look after six other siblings.
He left school at year 10, or the equivalent of, and then went into an apprenticeship.
I think he went to Vienna for an apprenticeship on-- he became a jeweler and a-- and a watchmaker.
And he was very proud of his trade.
And he was a good-- he was a good watchmaker and a good jeweler.
He was a good man.
And he remained that for the rest of his days, with the exception of during the path of when he was in the army, in the Czech army in Russia.
He was-- it's called [NON-ENGLISH] officer.
I don't know what that is.
He-- he-- he used to bring the provisions in, the food for the army or something like that.
Now before the war, what did he do?
As I said, he was-- he worked a lot.
He went to his little shop, and he, he did a lot of watchmaking.
And then he used to come home at lunch time and have a big, long siesta.
And then he'd go back to work and not come home till I was in bed.
So that's really-- I haven't got that much to say about him.
You were saying that your mother taught music.
Did you have a lot of music at home?
Well, you know these seven siblings of my father's, they turned out to be very creative.
And one sister was an opera singer.
And the other sister was a ballerina.
And my mother with the music and my father played the violin.
There was a [INAUDIBLE]-- there was a lot of music and a lot of expectations.
Of course, they all died, you know, but they was-- they used to think that I would be an opera singer.
And that was quite funny [LAUGHS] because I lost my voice by the time when I was 13.
I croaked.
So that was a bit of a disappointment.
Yes.
The ones that I remember was my, my-- both my grandfathers died before I was born, I think.
But my, my grandmothers were alive, and we had a lot of contact with them.
And then my mother's brother, Martin-- my mother came from Chatsa, which is a place in-- in Slovakia.
And my-- her brother was a-- was a chess champion.
He represented Czechoslovakia, and so on.
He perished, and the grandmother perished.
They just didn't come back after the war.
My-- then there was this huge family of my father's.
And they-- a lot of them lived in Ostrava.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What do I remember?
I remember that I was a very naughty child as a child.
And my mother's-- when my mother tried to say something nice about me she said, you're a naughty girl but you're not as naughty as your cousin Leanka.
So I remember Leanka.
Because she was naughtier than me, and that was pretty good.
What type of things did you do?
I have no idea, but I had this terrible reputation of throwing tantrums.
That's right.
I threw tantrums.
If I didn't get my way, I lay on the floor and kicked, kicked my heels.
And in fact, they was so-- oh, and really I was very self willed.
One year I decided that I wanted to visit my friend at the other end of Orlova, and I was really the boss of the governess.
And so it was rainy and stormy and they took me across.
And as a result, I caught pneumonia and nearly died.
And that's when my parents decided what I needed was a Prussian, you know, corporal who will tell me what to do.
And we got this German governess who-- and I learned German from her.
And she, she was such a bossy boots that I actually had to learn to do as I was told.
But then with all this stuff happening with Germany, she didn't want to work for Jews anymore.
And I remember driving her to the border and saying goodbye, and I was brokenhearted.
I couldn't understand why my dragon had to leave me.
So [DRINKS] I've lost my track.
What were you asking me?
You were talking about your extended family.
Oh, extended family.
What-- what did I do with them?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know.
Visited.
They visited us.
We visited them.
My father had a-- a brother who was extremely tall.
And he was a funny man.
He used to tease me.
I don't know whether that was-- no, no that was somebody else.
As I said, there were seven of them.
One ended up in Brazil.
So I-- I have some cousins running around Brazil someplace.
So they saved themselves.
And the others disappeared after the war.
They just didn't come back.
And what did I do with them?
I don't know.
I have a feeling of a house full of people.
I have a feeling of people visiting.
I have a feeling of chasing-- of people-- you know, kids chasing each other.
And that's all.
I don't really-- I can't remember sitting at a meal, or no.
You were saying that you were born in Vitkovice.
When did you move to Orlova?
I think we always lived in Orlova, and the hospital was in Vitkovice.
Well, you know, my memory's very sketchy.
I mean I can tell you that we lived in a-- at first we lived in-- in something small.
And I can't remember that at all, but my mother told me.
And I do remember moving to this really nice house.
That's when my parents obviously made enough money to buy this house, which had two stories.
But it actually was three stories.
Upstairs there was a dentist.
I think he was a terrible dentist, because he pulled two of my teeth quite unnecessarily.
And then we had the next floor down.
And then underneath in the-- in the cellar, somehow, there lived a poor family.
They had a lot of children.
And I remember I liked being with them.
But-- so my mother always sent a whole lot of food downstairs.
And I would come up and say, oh, isn't that funny.
We had exactly the same as you.
But ours was nicer.
So I remember that I really liked being there.
But it was in the cellar.
I can remember it was quite dark.
And, and, but they had lots of kids, and I was an only child.
So I liked it.
And I can't really remember-- I can remember-- not even remember, but I remember a photo of myself in my room.
It was quite light.
And there were these light curtains.
There was parquetry floor-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] can't remember.
I remember the garden.
There was a big garden, and there was a pond with ducks.
And there was a strawberry patch.
And I had a dog.
So that's all I can remember.
Well, I went to kindergarten-- and there's another story.
It's most embarrassing.
I must have been such a naughty girl.
My mother told me the story that at kindergarten there was a-- everybody was singing.
And that I marched across the room and smacked another girl.
And they said, why did you do that?
And, apparently I said, because she was singing off-key.
So, I had a very good ear, you know?
But that was my way of resolving things-- terrible.
So I obviously played with children in-- in kindergarten and so on, yeah.
Look, I have a feeling that they were all Jewish families.
I mean, I think the people in the cellar were Jewish, and the dentist upstairs was Jewish, and-- not the children in the kindergarten, I don't suppose.
But I don't know that we knew a lot of non-Jews.
There was quite a big Jewish population in Orlova.
I mean, I can't remember that.
But I remember my parents telling me.
Because they all-- they didn't survive, you see.
We were the only family that survived out of Orlova.
No.
I know there was a synagogue, and I know we went to the synagogue.
But no, I couldn't even guess-- hundreds, thousands, don't-- no idea.
Well, apart from being told not to say that I was Jewish, I know my mother, on Friday, she lit the candles.
And she put her hands in front of her face and, obviously, prayed.
But I-- I-- I didn't really understand what was going on.
I-- you know, I think we had a Friday Shabbas meal, I'm pretty sure.
But you know we're fairly atheistic sort of a family.
And during the war, my father was very angry with God.
God didn't do it right, you know?
And when I asked my mother-- I said to her-- when I grew up, I said to my mother, what were you doing when you had your, you know, hands in front of your face?
I never knew what you were doing.
She said, oh, I wasn't doing anything.
I said, well, why did you have it like that?
She said, because that's what my mother did.
So I think we lost the religion somewhere along the way.
But the tradition remained, at least bits of it.
Did you celebrate any Jewish holidays?
Well, I'm sure we did because I have a memory of apples with cloves stuck in them, and smell, and gefilte fish.
And I remember going to-- to-- to the synagogue, and my father having all this stuff on him.

So I'm sure we were-- we weren't out of the faith, but, as I said, just judging from after the war when my parents just both really disengaged, I don't know that-- Well, first of all we weren't kosher before the war, I think.
I'm pretty sure.
But there was-- I mean, I think that if it weren't for the war, we would've been really-- we would have remained, you know, within the community, which we didn't.
When?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, I want to say yes because the thing that I told you about.
But I-- there were some other incidents.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] If I can-- if I will-- if I think of something specific, I will let you know.
But my feeling is yes, and something about graffiti, something about, you know, keeping away.
And-- but it could have actually been-- because in Russia there was a lot of it, so I can't really answer this with any definitiveness.
Well, I can tell you when I got a bit older-- my memories from the age of eight are quite clear.
But before that, it's pretty hazy.
So if you ask me about Russia, I will be much clearer.
But-- and, and, you know, we got to Russia in '39, so-- I think.
So I just have to stay vague.
Sorry.
Yes, well, I've already mentioned that there was some kind of underground stuff happening.

And my mother must have been involved too, although-- because she must have known that those people were there, and that it was dangerous because the Poles and the Germans were really very cooperating at the time.
But I think my father was quite, you know, politically-minded and, you know, he had a strong sense of community whereas my mother was a little bit more individualistic at the time.
So were they involved?
Well, I know that my father-- I could never remember whether he was a Zionist or Bund.
But I think he was Bund because I never could understand exactly what that meant.
And that's why he didn't go to Israel, or I don't know, something like that.
So again I can only as a six-year-old-- I mean, you know, my granddaughter is now six, nearly seven.
If I-- if somebody asked her, you know, are your parents involved in X, she'd have terrible trouble when she's 64.
So I'm doing my best.
Did ?] you started school before the war started?
Yes.
You see, what happened was that-- I mean, the war started three times.
First it started in-- it was between the-- you know, we, we were in Czechoslovakia.
Next minute, we were in Poland.
We didn't cross any borders.
We were annexed.
Yes, because I can't say to you did I go to school.
Well, it depends on which bit of the war you're talking about.
I know I went to a Polish--
In Orlova, I may have gone for a tiny while to a Czech school, which I-- I don't know whether it was a kindergarten.
The next one was a Polish school, you see.
So we'll get to that one in a moment, huh?
OK.
Well, I can remember my parents being very worried.
I mean, there was a-- there was an atmosphere of, you know, sickening fear-- more my father than my mother.
My mother thought that he was a panic monger.
It'll all go away if you don't take any notice.
And nobody else was panicking.
And you know, we'll be all right.
But-- but there was-- there was a lot to of unrest.
There was a lot of, you know, fear about what's happening.
I mean, they were constantly listening to the news.
So.
Well, as a kid, you didn't sort of think about it.
You didn't-- [LAUGHS] I remember saying to my mother when we were running, are we chasing the Germans or are the Germans chasing us?
You know, you sort of lost track because you never knew where the front was and who was running where.
It was a confusing I think is probably the best way to describe it.
It was extremely confusing, because it didn't make sense.
You know, you get smacked if you're naughty.
You get punished if your bad.
What was happening?
Didn't have a clue.
But it made for a lot of anxiety.
Did your parents plan to leave in case something happened?
Well, my father wanted to go.
My father had this real sixth sense about self preservation.
And my mother didn't want to go because none of her friends were going.
But he said, we're going.
And he usually wasn't very bossy.
But this time he said, we're going.
And so he said, look, we're just pretend we're going on a holiday, and we'll pack the car.
And we'll just drive deeper into Poland.
We're not crossing any borders.
So he prepared.
That was just a little bit before the war broke out.
I mean, you will ask me how little.
I would say, looking at it now, a couple of months, say three month, four month-- wouldn't be anymore than that.
Orlova was Polish.
Orlova became Polish when the Germans and the Poles did some kind of a deal with Chamberlain and, you know, this-- I don't know who else was in it-- Chamberlain, Roosevelt?
Who knows?
Anyway, they sat around and they say, you have this bit.
You have this bit.
Now leave us alone-- except doesn't work that way.
[LAUGHS]
He just said, we will go on a holiday.
And he packed a few things, and we had a car.
And he took all these clocks and things, which got us into trouble later on.
So he packed up part of the shop.
And we locked up the house.
And we said, we're going away on holidays.
And we ended up in Krakow.
I always used to think it was Warsaw, but it wasn't.
It was Krakow because that's where the bombs started falling.
And that would have been '39, I suppose.
Well, we, I mean, the refugees were just fleeing, you know?
There were rivers of refugees.
And then there were rivers of these Polish soldiers that were fleeing.
And in the middle of this chaos, we met-- ran into my father's apprentice and his two elderly parents.
We're at this teensy-weensy little [NON-ENGLISH].
And, you know, mom and dad were in the front.
I was in the back.
And dad, he was very kind-hearted.
He took the two elderly people, stuck them in the back with me, and the boy, Lolac, was standing-- you know the old cars, they had like a step-- he was standing on that and holding on to the top.
And that's how we traveled a little-- a little further.
And then there was an incident where they were told that the Germans won't, won't hurt the women and children.
They will only hurt the men.
So there was a train.
And only the men were allowed to go on the train.
And we stood-- I remember that a little bit.
We stood in front of this platform, and my father decided, no, he wasn't going to leave his family.
But Lolac said he was going.
And I don't know what happened to the grandparents-- the old people.
They must have just perished.
But, of course, the train there was caught and the men were exterminated.
And the women would have, as we know now too.
So we basically saved ourselves because my father was such a-- he was brave.
And he was very committed.
He was very-- what's the word-- you know, his family was everything-- very Jewish, I think.
Not only Jewish, but also Jewish.
Now I don't know what you asked me.
Now finish-- we'll finish the tape.
Ah, OK.
